claim: Google's Android security data: memory-safety vulnerabilities as a share of total Android vulnerabilities fell from 76% (2019) to 35% (2022) to 24% (2024), well below the ~70% industry norm; absolute annual memory-safety vulns dropped from 223 (2019) to 85 (2022), with 2024 extrapolated to ~36 (27 after the Sept bulletin). This is the strongest production evidence for the 'new code in a memory-safe language' strategy.
evidence: 2024 primary source (https://security.googleblog.com/2024/09/eliminating-memory-safety-vulnerabilities-Android.html, "Eliminating Memory Safety Vulnerabilities at the Source," September 25, 2024): "Memory safety issues, which accounted for 76% of Android vulnerabilities in 2019, and are currently 24% in 2024, well below the 70% industry norm, and continuing to drop." and "Note that the data for 2024 is extrapolated to the full year (represented as 36, but currently at 27 after the September security bulletin)." | 2022 alternate source (https://security.googleblog.com/2022/12/memory-safe-languages-in-android-13.html, "Memory Safe Languages in Android 13," December 1, 2022): "From 2019 to 2022 the annual number of memory safety vulnerabilities dropped from 223 down to 85." and "From 2019 to 2022 it has dropped from 76% down to 35% of Android's total vulnerabilities."

2024 blog: "Memory safety issues, which accounted for 76% of Android vulnerabilities in 2019, and are currently 24% in 2024, well below the 70% industry norm, and continuing to drop." and "Note that the data for 2024 is extrapolated to the full year (represented as 36, but currently at 27 after the September security bulletin)." | 2022 blog: "From 2019 to 2022 the annual number of memory safety vulnerabilities dropped from 223 down to 85." and "From 2019 to 2022 it has dropped from 76% down to 35% of Android's total vulnerabilities."
corrections: All numeric figures are exactly correct and explicitly supported: 76% (2019), 35% (2022), 24% (2024); ~70% industry norm; 223 (2019) and 85 (2022) absolute vulns; 2024 extrapolated to 36, currently 27 after the September bulletin. The only non-sourced element is the editorial superlative "strongest production evidence for the 'new code in a memory-safe language' strategy" — this is the claimant's interpretive framing rather than a source quote, but it does not contradict the sources, which themselves argue the focus should be on "new code, not rewriting existing components" and that "Safe Coding for new code... reduces the overall security risk." No factual overclaim found.

claim: Biscuit (MIT, OSDI 2018, Cutler et al.) is a research POSIX kernel of ~28,000 lines of Go + 1,546 lines of assembly, no C, implementing 58 syscalls and two real device drivers; on kernel-intensive benchmarks (NGINX, Redis) the fraction of kernel CPU time spent on HLL features (GC + thread-stack-expansion checks) ranges 'up to 13%' - quantifying the GC/HLL tax for an in-kernel POSIX kernel.
evidence: Abstract: "On a set of kernel-intensive benchmarks (including NGINX and Redis) the fraction of kernel CPU time Biscuit spends on HLL features (primarily garbage collection and thread stack expansion checks) ranges up to 13%. ... Biscuit implements two significant device drivers in Go: one for AHCI SATA disk controllers and one for Intel 82599-based Ethernet controllers. Biscuit has nearly 28 thousand lines of Go, 1546 lines of assembler, and no C." Section 7 (Implementation): "The Biscuit kernel is written almost entirely in Go: Figure 3 shows that it has 27,583 lines of Go, 1,546 lines of assembly, and no C. ... Biscuit provides 58 system calls, listed in Figure 4." Title page: "The benefits and costs of writing a POSIX kernel in a high-level language. Cody Cutler, M. Frans Kaashoek, and Robert T. Morris, MIT CSAIL ... 13th USENIX Symposium on Operating Systems Design and Implementation (OSDI '18). October 8-10, 2018."

From the abstract/intro: "On a set of kernel-intensive benchmarks (including NGINX and Redis) the fraction of kernel CPU time Biscuit spends on HLL features (primarily garbage collection and thread stack expansion checks) ranges up to 13%." and "Biscuit implements two significant device drivers in Go: one for AHCI SATA disk controllers and one for Intel 82599-based Ethernet controllers. Biscuit has nearly 28 thousand lines of Go, 1546 lines of assembler, and no C." Authors line: "Cody Cutler, M. Frans Kaashoek, Robert T. Morris, MIT CSAIL" in "13th USENIX Symposium on Operating Systems Design and Implementation (OSDI '18)." Body: "Figure 3 shows that it has 27,583 lines of Go, 1,546 lines of assembly, and no C. Biscuit provides 58 system calls, listed in Figure 4."
corrections: All numbers, dates, and the document name check out exactly: ~28,000 lines of Go (27,583, i.e. "nearly 28 thousand"), 1,546 lines of assembly, no C, 58 system calls, two device drivers (AHCI SATA + Intel 82599 Ethernet), HLL-feature kernel-CPU fraction "up to 13%" on kernel-intensive benchmarks including NGINX and Redis, by Cutler/Kaashoek/Morris (MIT CSAIL) at OSDI '18 (2018). One trivial wording nuance: the paper calls them "two significant device drivers" rather than "two real device drivers," but this is an accurate paraphrase (both are functional drivers for real hardware) and not a numerical/date error. Verdict stands as confirmed.

No correction needed; every detail is supported. Minor notes: the precise Go count is 27,583 (the paper rounds it to "nearly 28 thousand," consistent with the claim's "~28,000"); the paper says "significant" device drivers (claim's "real" is a faithful paraphrase); and the paper qualifies HLL features as "primarily" GC and thread-stack-expansion checks (i.e., these dominate but are not the only HLL costs). The "up to 13%" figure is quoted verbatim with no stretch.

claim: Asterinas (arXiv:2506.03876, submitted 2025-06-04, USENIX ATC '25) is a Linux-ABI-compatible 'framekernel' that confines unsafe Rust to a privileged OS Framework (OSTD) of approximately 15,000 LOC = 14% of the 100,000+ LOC kernel, with OS Services written 100% in safe Rust and performance comparable to Linux - addressing 'improper use of unsafe Rust in kernel development.'
evidence: arXiv abstract (2506.03876): "Existing Rust-based OSes fall short due to their improper use of unsafe Rust in kernel development... we propose a novel OS architecture called framekernel... We present OSTD... and Asterinas, a Linux ABI-compatible framekernel OS implemented entirely in safe Rust using OSTD. Supporting over 210 Linux system calls, Asterinas delivers performance on par with Linux, while maintaining a minimized, memory-safety TCB of only about 14.0% of the codebase." Submission history: "[v1] Wed, 4 Jun 2025 12:10:26 UTC". Blog (asterinas.github.io, 2025/06/04): "to be published at USENIX ATC 2025"; "over 100,000 lines of Rust"; "Our TCB (OSTD) is approximately 15,000 lines of code—just 14% of the whole Asterinas kernel"; "Asterinas contributors build features in 100% safe Rust."

arXiv abstract (2506.03876): "Existing Rust-based OSes fall short due to their improper use of unsafe Rust in kernel development. To address this challenge, we propose a novel OS architecture called framekernel... We present OSTD, a streamlined framework for safe Rust OS development, and Asterinas, a Linux ABI-compatible framekernel OS implemented entirely in safe Rust using OSTD. Supporting over 210 Linux system calls, Asterinas delivers performance on par with Linux, while maintaining a minimized, memory-safety TCB of only about 14.0% of the codebase." Submission header: "[Submitted on 4 Jun 2025]". Blog (asterinas.github.io, 2025-06-04): "This post distills our research paper Asterinas... which is to be published at USENIX ATC 2025." / "Our TCB (OSTD) is approximately 15,000 lines of code—just 14% of the whole Asterinas kernel." / "After three years of development and over 100,000 lines of Rust..." / "Only OSTD developers touch unsafe, which is thoroughly reviewed and verified." / "Asterinas contributors build features in 100% safe Rust." / "The de-privileged OS services, written entirely in safe Rust atop the framework's abstractions, implement the bulk of OS functionality."
corrections: No correction needed — every component is explicitly supported. Minor non-defect note on the overclaim lens: the source's own arithmetic is slightly loose (15,000 LOC of "over 100,000" lines is nominally ~15%, yet the source states 14%), but the claim faithfully reproduces both figures the source publishes (approx. 15,000 LOC and 14%/14.0%), so it does not stretch beyond the source. Likewise, the blog scopes "performance on par with Linux" to single-core LMbench microbenchmarks (multi-core scalability "actively being improved"), but the unqualified abstract statement "Asterinas delivers performance on par with Linux" supports the claim's "performance comparable to Linux."

claim: Fuchsia's Programming Language Policy (last updated 2025-02-28) is an explicit allowlist: Rust is 'approved for use throughout the Fuchsia Platform Source Tree' EXCEPT the kernel; the Zircon kernel is C++ and 'built using a restricted set of technologies that have established industry track records of being used in production operating systems'; garbage-collected languages are banned from production after measured failure.
evidence: From https://fuchsia.dev/fuchsia-src/contribute/governance/policy/programming_languages — Footer: "Last updated 2025-02-28 UTC." Title: "Fuchsia Programming Language Policy." Body: "This policy is an allowlist. Languages not explicitly allowed below are considered not allowed." Rust Decision: "Rust is approved for use throughout the Fuchsia Platform Source Tree, with the following exceptions: kernel. The Zircon kernel is built using a restricted set of technologies that have established industry track records of being used in production operating systems."

From the policy (last updated 2025-02-28). Rust: "Rust is approved for use throughout the Fuchsia Platform Source Tree, with the following exceptions: kernel. The Zircon kernel is built using a restricted set of technologies that have established industry track records of being used in production operating systems." On garbage-collected languages, the policy does NOT impose a blanket ban: Go — "Go is not approved, with the following exceptions: netstack." with the rationale "The system components the Fuchsia project has built in Go have used more memory and kernel resources than their counterparts (or replacements) the Fuchsia project has built using C++ or Rust." Dart — "use of Dart is allowlisted in the Fuchsia Platform Source Tree, and new additions to the allowlist require an exemption." Python — "Python 3 is approved for use in the Fuchsia Platform Source Tree for the following purposes: Build, Host tools, System testing." On the kernel's language, the policy says only "C is approved for use in the Fuchsia Platform Source Tree: for low-level systems programming, including within the kernel"; neither the policy nor the kernel concepts page states the Zircon kernel "is C++."
corrections: All figures, dates and document names in the claim are exactly right: the date 2025-02-28 matches the page footer verbatim, the document title is "Fuchsia Programming Language Policy," and both quoted phrases ("approved for use throughout the Fuchsia Platform Source Tree" and the kernel "restricted set of technologies..." sentence) are verbatim. Minor non-numeric caveat outside this lens: neither source explicitly states "the Zircon kernel is C++" — the policy lists both C ("approved... including within the kernel") and C++ ("approved... throughout the Platform Source Tree"), and the kernel-concepts page (last updated 2025-09-26) names no language; this is a characterization, not a number/date/name error. Likewise "garbage-collected languages are banned from production after measured failure" is a fair paraphrase (Go "not approved" except netstack citing measured higher memory/kernel-resource use; Dart allowlisted via RFC-0176; Python only for build/host tools/system testing) rather than a verbatim quote, but involves no figure or date discrepancy.

The accurate spine of the claim checks out verbatim: the policy is an explicit allowlist ("Languages not explicitly allowed below are considered not allowed"), was last updated 2025-02-28, Rust is "approved for use throughout the Fuchsia Platform Source Tree" with the sole exception of the kernel, and the restricted-technologies sentence is quoted correctly. But two appended details overclaim. (1) "the Zircon kernel is C++" is not asserted by either source. The policy approves C "including within the kernel" and approves C++ "throughout the Platform Source Tree," but never states the kernel is written in C++; the kernel concepts page does not name a language at all. (2) "garbage-collected languages are banned from production after measured failure" overgeneralizes. The "measured failure" rationale (more memory/kernel resources than C++/Rust counterparts) is stated specifically for Go, not for GC languages as a class. The policy does not uniformly ban GC languages: Dart (GC) is allowlisted in the Platform Source Tree (with exemptions) and supported for end-developers targeting non-drivers — its decision cites maintenance burden, not a measured production failure; Python (GC) is approved for Build, Host tools, and System testing. Only Go is effectively excluded from production (and even then netstack is exempted). So the blanket "GC banned from production after measured failure" stretches a Go-specific finding into a category-wide ban that the source does not state.

claim: Klein et al. (ACM TOCS 32(1), Feb 2014) report seL4 kernel development took 2.2 person-years and the functional-correctness proof ~20 py (20.5 py total: ~9 py reusable frameworks/tools + 11 py seL4-specific) for ~10,000 lines of verified C; total cost $362/SLOC ($127/SLOC to replicate). seL4 remains the first and still only general-purpose OS kernel with a code-level functional-correctness proof (extending to the binary on ARM/RISC-V), with zero functional-correctness defects since 2009.
evidence: Primary source (Klein et al., 7371.pdf): header "Comprehensive Formal Verification of an OS Microkernel ... ACM Trans. Comput. Syst. 32, 1, Article 2 (February 2014), 70 pages." | "While the effort of the whole seL4 microkernel development, including design, documentation, coding, and testing, was 2.2 person-years (py), the total effort of the seL4 correctness proof was about 20 py: 9 py went into the formal frameworks and tools, and 11 py on the seL4-specific proof (roughly 8 py for the first refinement, including invariants, and 3 py for the second)." | Figure 19(b): "Correctness Proof Effort 20.5 py ... 9 py Generic framework & tools." | "The total of 20 py for the proofs includes significant research and about 9 py invested in formal language frameworks, proof tools, proof automation, theorem prover extensions and libraries. The total effort for the seL4-specific proof was 11 py." | "Our total cost per SLOC is $362 for the whole 22.7 py; it would be $127/SLOC for the estimated 8 py involved in replicating it for a new kernel." | "It is also the only evolving formally-verified code base of the order of 10 000 lines of code." ALTERNATE FAQ (sel4.systems/About/FAQ.html): "comprise the first (and still only) general-purpose OS kernel with a full code-level functional correctness proof ... On the ARM and RISCV64 platforms, there is a further proof that the binary code which executes on the hardware is a correct translation of the C code ... extends the functional correctness property to the binary." ALTERNATE proofs.html: "there have been no functional correctness defects in verified code since the functional correctness proof completed in 2009 ... in the entire 10,000 lines of verified code nothing has gone wrong in all that time."

Primary (7371.pdf, Klein et al., ACM TOCS 32(1), Feb 2014): "the effort of the whole seL4 microkernel development, including design, documentation, coding, and testing, was 2.2 person-years (py), the total effort of the seL4 correctness proof was about 20 py: 9 py went into the formal frameworks and tools, and 11 py on the seL4-specific proof" — Figure 19 labels "Development Effort 2.2 py" and "Correctness Proof Effort ... Total Effort 20.5 py ... Generic framework & tools 9 py." Cost: "Our total cost per SLOC is $362 for the whole 22.7 py; it would be $127/SLOC for the estimated 8 py involved in replicating it for a new kernel." Size: "the only evolving formally-verified code base of the order of 10 000 lines of code." FAQ: "comprise the first (and still only) general-purpose OS kernel with a full code-level functional correctness proof" and "On the ARM and RISCV64 platforms, there is a further proof that the binary code which executes on the hardware is a correct translation of the C code ... extends the functional correctness property to the binary." proofs.html: "there have been no functional correctness defects in verified code since the functional correctness proof completed in 2009."
corrections: No factual error. Two minor framing notes (not corrections to verdict): (1) The paper writes "Generic framework & tools," not literally "reusable" — the claim's "reusable" is a fair paraphrase justified by the paper's replication argument. (2) The figure's 20.5 py total vs. the text's "9 py + 11 py = ~20 py" reflects the paper's own rounding (seL4-specific components sum to ~11.5 py); the claim correctly cites both "~20 py" and "20.5 py total."

claim: VeriSMo (Zhou et al., Microsoft Research, OSDI'24) is the first verified security module for confidential VMs (AMD SEV-SNP), written in Rust and verified with Verus for functional correctness, secure information flow, and VM confidentiality/integrity under a fully adversarial hypervisor; only 31 LOC of unsafe Rust for hardware primitives are trusted, the proof-and-spec-to-code ratio is ~2:1, and verification runs in minutes - evidence that Rust+Verus collapses verification cost versus C+Isabelle.
evidence: Confirmations (verbatim): "we present VERISMO1, the first verified security module for confidential VMs on AMD SEV-SNP." / "VERISMO is implemented in Rust and verified using Verus [22]" / "VERISMO is fully verified for functional correctness, secure information flow, and VM confidentiality and integrity." / "We only used 31 LOC unsafe Rust for hardware primitive operations." / "On average, the proof and specification annotations are approximately twice the size of the executable code for the implementation layer." / "It takes roughly 6 minutes to verify VERISMO on a 32-core machine". The paper's only C comparison is about performance, not verification cost: "When compared with a C-based implementation, VERISMO achieves similar performance." and "Due to the nature of unsafe C, however, their memory safety proofs require more effort than proofs about Rust."

Abstract/intro confirm the factual core: "we introduce VERISMO, the first verified security module for confidential VMs on AMD SEV-SNP. VERISMO is fully functional... VERISMO is fully verified for functional correctness, secure information flow, and VM confidentiality and integrity. The key challenge in verifying VERISMO is that the untrusted hypervisor can interrupt VERISMO's execution and modify the hardware state at any time"; "implemented in Rust and verified using Verus [22]". Sec 8.3: "We only used 31 LOC unsafe Rust for hardware primitive operations"; "On average, the proof and specification annotations are approximately twice the size of the executable code for the implementation layer"; "Verification of both model and implementation layers takes around 6 minutes with a multi-threaded Verus backed by Z3-4.11.2... running on our test machine with 32 cores." BUT the final clause overclaims. The paper's C comparison is about PERFORMANCE, not verification cost: "We compared VERISMO with a C-based implementation and observed similar performance." The only effort statement (Related Work) is qualitative: "Due to the nature of unsafe C, however, their memory safety proofs require more effort than proofs about Rust." The string "Isabelle" does not appear anywhere in the paper (grep: 0 hits), and no head-to-head verification-cost comparison to a C+Isabelle system is made.
corrections:
Every figure, date, and core descriptor in the claim is exactly right per the OSDI'24 paper (https://www.usenix.org/system/files/osdi24-zhou.pdf): first verified security module for confidential VMs on AMD SEV-SNP; Rust+Verus; functional correctness, secure information flow, and VM confidentiality/integrity under an untrusted/malicious concurrent hypervisor; exactly "31 LOC unsafe Rust for hardware primitive operations" trusted; proof+spec "approximately twice the size of the executable code" (~2:1); and verification "around 6 minutes" on a 32-core machine (Z3-4.11.2). Authors/venue (Zhou et al., lead author Microsoft Research, OSDI'24) are correct.

The single inaccurate detail is the closing framing "evidence that Rust+Verus collapses verification cost versus C+Isabelle." The word "Isabelle" does not appear anywhere in the paper (0 occurrences), and "seL4" appears only as two bibliography citations, not as a comparison baseline. The paper makes NO quantitative verification-cost comparison against any C+Isabelle (e.g., seL4-style) effort. Its only C comparison is a runtime-performance comparison ("similar performance"; the "c-sm" prototype supports only WakeUpAP/SetPageShared/SetPagePrivate and is explicitly a performance baseline). The paper offers only a qualitative remark in Related Work that unsafe-C memory-safety proofs "require more effort than proofs about Rust" — it neither names Isabelle nor quantifies a "collapse" in verification cost, and the 6-minute figure is presented as evidence of Verus/Z3 proof efficiency, not as a head-to-head against C+Isabelle. So the "C+Isabelle" comparison is a fabricated document-name detail not supported by the source.

The technical sub-claims are all correct (first verified SEV-SNP security module; Rust + Verus; functional correctness + secure information flow + VM confidentiality/integrity under an untrusted/malicious concurrent hypervisor; 31 LOC trusted unsafe Rust for hardware primitives; proof+spec roughly 2x executable code for the implementation layer; ~6 min verification on a 32-core machine). However, the concluding editorial clause "evidence that Rust+Verus collapses verification cost versus C+Isabelle" overclaims beyond the source. The paper never mentions Isabelle (nor Coq/HOL) and makes no verification-cost comparison against any C+Isabelle system. Its only C comparison concerns runtime PERFORMANCE ("similar performance," "no performance trade-off"), using an unverified C baseline (c-sm). The single effort-related statement is a qualitative remark that unsafe-C "memory safety proofs require more effort than proofs about Rust" - not a quantified "collapse" of verification cost, and not tied to Isabelle. Two further nuances: the ~2:1 ratio is stated specifically for the implementation layer (not the whole system, where Table 2 shows much larger proof:exec ratios), and "31 LOC unsafe" is a distinct metric from the separately reported "32 unsafe blocks."

claim: Rust first merged into mainline Linux in v6.1 (Dec 2022) as infrastructure; the Android Binder driver (carrying the majority of Android IPC) was rewritten in Rust and merged into Linux v6.18-rc1 - production evidence of Rust in a security/performance-critical kernel subsystem.
evidence: From https://rust-for-linux.com/android-binder-driver: "This project is an effort to rewrite Android's Binder kernel driver in Rust. The driver was merged into Linux kernel version v6.18-rc1." ... "Binder is one of the most security and performance critical components of Android." ... "The majority of inter-process communication (IPC) on Android goes through Binder." From https://kernelnewbies.org/Linux_6.1: "Linux 6.1 was released on Sun, 11 December 2022. Summary: This release includes initial support for the Rust programming language".

From https://rust-for-linux.com/android-binder-driver: "This project is an effort to rewrite Android's Binder kernel driver in Rust. The driver was merged into Linux kernel version v6.18-rc1." / "Binder is one of the most security and performance critical components of Android." / "The majority of inter-process communication (IPC) on Android goes through Binder." — AND from https://kernelnewbies.org/Linux_6.1: "Linux 6.1 was released on Sun, 11 December 2022. Summary: This release includes initial support for the Rust programming language."
corrections: No correction needed; claim does not overclaim. Minor sourcing note: the v6.1/December-2022 "first merged as infrastructure" detail is NOT stated on rust-for-linux.com itself (its homepage only lists Binder among "Users — in mainline" without dates); it was independently verified via kernelnewbies.org/Linux_6.1, which describes it as "initial support for the Rust programming language" in the 6.1 release dated 11 December 2022. All four sub-claims (v6.1 infrastructure debut, Binder rewrite, majority of Android IPC, merge in v6.18-rc1, security/performance-critical subsystem) are supported by explicit source text with no stretch.

claim: Ferrocene is a qualified Rust toolchain: qualified for ISO 26262 up to ASIL D (the highest risk class) and IEC 61508 (qualified SIL 3, supports SIL 4), originating from the Oct 29 2023 TUV SUD qualification of Ferrocene 23.06.0 (Rust 1.68, the first qualification of a Rust compiler); it additionally covers IEC 62304 Class C (medical) and supports DO-178C DAL C (aerospace).
evidence: PRIMARY (https://ferrocene.dev/en/): "Ferrocene is qualified to be used in standard-compliant development projects, according to ISO 26262 (ASIL D), the highest risk class, for peace of mind." | "Ferrocene is qualified under IEC 61508 (SIL 3) and supports customer certification efforts for SIL 4 compliance." | "Ferrocene is qualified for use in safety-related medical development projects according to IEC 62304 (Class C)." | "support customer certification efforts for DO-178C (DAL C) compliance." ALTERNATE (ferrous-systems.com): "Ferrocene 23.06.0, based on Rust 1.68, is now fully usable in safety critical environments... TÜV SÜD officially sent over the certification on Monday October 29. This is the first qualification of a Rust compiler." (Year 2023 fixed by version 23.06.0 and the "early 2024" rollout references; ASIL D: "up to ASIL D – the highest classification of initial hazard.")

Primary source (ferrocene.dev/en/): "Ferrocene is qualified to be used in standard-compliant development projects, according to ISO 26262 (ASIL D), the highest risk class" ... "Ferrocene is qualified under IEC 61508 (SIL 3) and supports customer certification efforts for SIL 4 compliance." ... "Ferrocene is qualified for use in safety-related medical development projects according to IEC 62304 (Class C)." ... "Ferrocene's qualified Rust compiler is ready to provide aerospace teams with the tooling and guidance necessary to support customer certification efforts for DO-178C (DAL C) compliance." Alternate (ferrous-systems.com blog): "Ferrocene 23.06.0, based on Rust 1.68, is now fully usable in safety critical environments." ... "TÜV SÜD officially sent over the certification on Monday October 29. This is the first qualification of a Rust compiler." ... "We've qualified Ferrocene for use in systems up to ASIL D – the highest classification of initial hazard as defined by this standard."
corrections: No correction needed. All figures, dates, and document names match the sources exactly. One context note: the 2023 blog originally announced IEC 61508 "qualified it at SIL4," whereas the current ferrocene.dev primary source states "qualified under IEC 61508 (SIL 3) and supports ... SIL 4" — the claim correctly follows the primary source's SIL 3-qualified / SIL 4-supported wording rather than the older blog phrasing.

No correction needed; every component is explicitly supported and the claim does not overclaim. Two trivial, non-load-bearing notes (neither affects the claim, which omits both details): (1) the blog's word "Monday October 29" is internally inaccurate — October 29, 2023 was actually a Sunday; the claim's "Oct 29 2023" date is otherwise correct and the year is unambiguous from context (Rust 1.68 = March 2023). (2) The claim's choice of "IEC 61508 (qualified SIL 3, supports SIL 4)" follows the current primary-source language rather than the older blog's stronger "qualified it at SIL4," which is the more conservative and accurate phrasing — not an overclaim.

claim: Rust has no MISRA equivalent and no ISO standard; the real safety-critical stack is the Ferrocene Language Specification (FLS, created July 2022 with AdaCore for compiler qualification), donated to the Rust Project on Mar 26 2025 to become (with the Rust Reference) the official authoritative specification, plus the Safety-Critical Rust Consortium (founded June 12 2024) FLS-anchored coding guidelines.
evidence: Rust blog (https://blog.rust-lang.org/2025/03/26/adopting-the-fls/, dated "Mar. 26, 2025"): "Some years ago, Ferrous Systems and AdaCore worked together to assemble a description of Rust called the FLS... they've successfully used it to qualify toolchains based on Rust for use in safety-critical industries." Footnote: "The FLS stood for the 'Ferrocene Language Specification'." Rust Foundation (https://rustfoundation.org/media/announcing-the-safety-critical-rust-consortium/): "DOVER, DELAWARE, USA, June 12, 2024 – ... thrilled to jointly announce the Safety-Critical Rust Consortium." Ferrous Systems "Introducing the Ferrocene Language Specification" (dated 2022-06-01): "our work to qualify rustc to produce Ferrocene... a key part of our partnership with AdaCore... we aim to publish an initial draft in Summer 2022."

From the primary source (blog.rust-lang.org, Mar. 26, 2025): "we're pleased to announce that we'll be adopting the FLS into the Rust Project as part of our ongoing specification efforts." And under "What's this mean for the Rust Reference?": "The Reference is still the Reference. Adopting the FLS does not change the status of the Reference, and we plan to continue to improve and expand the Reference as we've been doing. ... in the long term, we're hopeful we can find ways to bring these two documents closer together." On origin: "Some years ago, Ferrous Systems and AdaCore worked together to assemble a description of Rust called the FLS ... they've successfully used it to qualify toolchains based on Rust for use in safety-critical industries." Footnote: "The FLS stood for the 'Ferrocene Language Specification'." Consortium source (Rust Foundation): "DOVER, DELAWARE, USA, June 12, 2024 – ... are thrilled to jointly announce the Safety-Critical Rust Consortium."
corrections: On the numbers-and-dates axis the claim holds: document names ("Ferrocene Language Specification"/FLS, "Rust Reference") are correct; "Mar 26 2025" is the exact adoption-announcement date; "June 12 2024" is the exact Consortium announcement date. One date is loose: no source states "July 2022" in prose. The FLS GitHub repo and first commits date to 2022-05-11, the work was publicly announced 2022-06-01 ("aim to publish an initial draft in Summer 2022"), and the first archived public version of the spec (spec.ferrocene.dev) is 2022-07-26 — so "July 2022" matches the first public release but the document's creation/announcement predates it by ~1-2 months. Two non-date characterizations (outside this lens) are not supported: the Rust blog explicitly states "The Reference is still the Reference. Adopting the FLS does not change the status of the Reference," contradicting "becomes the official authoritative specification"; and the Consortium announcement never mentions the FLS, so the "FLS-anchored coding guidelines" descriptor is not backed by that source.

The claim overclaims and misdates in several places. (1) The biggest stretch: the source does NOT say the FLS, together with the Rust Reference, becomes "the official authoritative specification." The blog explicitly states the opposite framing — "Adopting the FLS does not change the status of the Reference" — and describes the move as "part of our ongoing specification efforts," with FLS and Reference remaining two separate documents the Project only "hope[s]" to "bring ... closer together" in the long term. There is no declaration of a single official authoritative spec. (2) "created July 2022" is unsupported by any fetched source; the blog says only "Some years ago." (3) The Mar 26 2025 event is described as "adopting"/"bring it into the Project" (made possible by Ferrous Systems), not literally "donated" — though that wording is close. (4) "FLS-anchored coding guidelines" is not supported by the consortium announcement, which lists "guidelines" only among many possible deliverables (linters, libraries, static analysis, formal methods, language subsets) without tying them to the FLS. (5) The "no MISRA equivalent and no ISO standard" assertion is not addressed by any of these sources. Verifiably correct details: FLS = Ferrocene Language Specification, co-created with AdaCore, used for toolchain qualification, adopted by the Rust Project on Mar 26 2025, and the Safety-Critical Rust Consortium founded June 12 2024.

claim: CISA/FBI 'Product Security Bad Practices' (v1.0 October 2024; v2.0 January 2025, which added context to the memory safety section) names as Bad Practice #1 (Product Properties): 'The development of new product lines for use in service of critical infrastructure or NCFs in a memory-unsafe language (e.g., C or C++) where readily available alternative memory-safe languages could be used'; manufacturers are urged to publish a memory-safety roadmap by end-2025.
evidence: Change Record: "1.0 | October 2024 | Initial Version" and "2.0 | January 2025 | ... Additional context added to the memory safety section." Product Properties item 1: "The development of new product lines for use in service of critical infrastructure or NCFs in a memory-unsafe language (e.g., C or C++) where readily available alternative memory-safe languages could be used is dangerous and significantly elevates risk to national security..." Recommended action: "For existing products, software manufacturers should publish a memory safety roadmap by the end of 2025, outlining their prioritized approach to eliminating memory safety vulnerabilities..." Authorship: "CISA and the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI)—hereafter referred to as the authoring organizations—developed this guidance."

Change Record: "1.0 | October 2024 | Initial Version" and "2.0 | January 2025 | ... Additional context added to the memory safety section." Authoring orgs: "CISA and the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI)." Under Product Properties, item 1: "The development of new product lines for use in service of critical infrastructure or NCFs in a memory-unsafe language (e.g., C or C++) where readily available alternative memory-safe languages could be used is dangerous and significantly elevates risk to national security, national economic security, and national public health and safety." Recommended action: "For existing products, software manufacturers should publish a memory safety roadmap by the end of 2025, outlining their prioritized approach to eliminating memory safety vulnerabilities in priority code components written in memory unsafe languages."

claim: NSA Cybersecurity Information Sheet 'Software Memory Safety' (doc U/OO/219936-22, PP-23-0782; original 10 Nov 2022, updated APR 2023 Ver. 1.1) states verbatim: 'NSA recommends using a memory safe language when possible' and advises organizations 'to consider making a strategic shift from programming languages that provide little or no inherent memory safety' (such as C/C++) to memory-safe languages.
evidence: "NSA recommends using a memory safe language when possible." ... "NSA advises organizations to consider making a strategic shift from programming languages that provide little or no inherent memory protection, such as C/C++ and assembly, to a memory safe language when possible." (NSA CSI "Software Memory Safety," footer: "U/OO/219936-22 | PP-23-0782 | APR 2023 Ver. 1.1")

Executive summary: "NSA recommends using a memory safe language when possible." The path forward: "NSA advises organizations to consider making a strategic shift from programming languages that provide little or no inherent memory protection, such as C/C++ and assembly, to a memory safe language when possible." Footer (all 7 pages): "U/OO/219936-22 | PP-23-0782 | APR 2023 Ver. 1.1"
corrections: All document identifiers and dates in the claim are exactly correct: doc U/OO/219936-22, PP-23-0782, version "APR 2023 Ver. 1.1," title "Software Memory Safety," and original publication 10 Nov 2022 (confirmed by the media.defense.gov URL path /2022/Nov/10/). The first verbatim quote ("NSA recommends using a memory safe language when possible") is also exactly correct. However, the SECOND quoted phrase is misquoted. The claim renders it as "...little or no inherent memory SAFETY," but the source actually reads "...little or no inherent memory PROTECTION." The source also names "C/C++ and assembly" (the claim drops "and assembly," though that parenthetical is not inside the verbatim quotation marks). Because the claim presents a misquoted phrase as a verbatim quotation (safety vs. protection), the claim is refuted on that wording detail. Note: the primary media.defense.gov URL returned HTTP "Access Denied" to direct fetch; verification was performed against the Wayback Machine archive (20260531164157), which is a valid 7-page PDF of the v1.1 document.

Mostly accurate but the second quotation is misquoted at the word level, so it fails a strict verbatim check. Confirmed: the doc identifiers (U/OO/219936-22, PP-23-0782, APR 2023 Ver. 1.1; original 10 Nov 2022 per the media.defense.gov /2022/Nov/10/ path), and the verbatim sentence "NSA recommends using a memory safe language when possible." However, the source says the strategic shift is from languages with "little or no inherent memory PROTECTION" — not "memory safety" as the claim's single-quoted (presented-as-verbatim) phrase states. The source also lists the examples as "such as C/C++ and assembly" (the claim drops "assembly"). The substance/thesis of the claim is correct; the specific quoted wording is off ("protection" -> "safety"). NOTE: the primary media.defense.gov URL returned HTTP 403; verification was performed against the supplied Wayback Machine alternate (web.archive.org capture 20260531164157), which returned the full 7-page PDF (HTTP 200).

claim: The EU Cyber Resilience Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/2847) entered into force 10 Dec 2024; vulnerability/incident reporting obligations apply from 11 Sep 2026 and main manufacturer obligations from 11 Dec 2027; manufacturers must draw up a machine-readable SBOM covering at least the top-level dependencies, an OS plus hypervisor is a regulated 'important product', and non-compliance with essential cybersecurity requirements carries penalties up to EUR 15 million or 2.5% of worldwide annual turnover.
evidence:
DOCUMENT NAME/NUMBER (EUR-Lex legal text): "REGULATION (EU) 2024/2847 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL of 23 October 2024 on horizontal cybersecurity requirements for products with digital elements ... (Cyber Resilience Act)".

ENTRY INTO FORCE + APPLICATION DATES (Primary source, digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu): "The CRA entered into force on 10 December 2024. The main obligations introduced by the Act will apply from 11 December 2027, with reporting obligations to apply as of 11 September 2026." Corroborated by Article 71 (EUR-Lex): "This Regulation shall enter into force on the twentieth day following that of its publication ... This Regulation shall apply from 11 December 2027. However, Article 14 shall apply from 11 September 2026 and Chapter IV (Articles 35 to 51) shall apply from 11 June 2026." Recital confirms Article 14 covers "reporting obligations concerning actively exploited vulnerabilities and severe incidents ... which should apply from 11 September 2026".

MACHINE-READABLE SBOM (Annex I, Part II, point (1), EUR-Lex): "identify and document vulnerabilities and components contained in products with digital elements, including by drawing up a software bill of materials in a commonly used and machine-readable format covering at the very least the top-level dependencies of the products".

IMPORTANT PRODUCTS - OS + HYPERVISOR (Annex III, EUR-Lex): Class I includes "11. Operating systems"; Class II includes "1. Hypervisors and container runtime systems that support virtualised execution of operating systems and similar environments". Both are listed among the "important products with digital elements".

PENALTIES (Article 64(2), EUR-Lex): "Non-compliance with the essential cybersecurity requirements set out in Annex I and the obligations set out in Articles 13 and 14 shall be subject to administrative fines of up to EUR 15 000 000 or, if the offender is an undertaking, up to 2,5 % of the its total worldwide annual turnover for the preceding financial year, whichever is higher."

DATES (primary source, digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu): "The CRA entered into force on 10 December 2024. The main obligations introduced by the Act will apply from 11 December 2027, with reporting obligations to apply as of 11 September 2026." | SBOM (EUR-Lex OJ:L_202402847, Annex I Part II 'Vulnerability handling requirements', point (1)): "identify and document vulnerabilities and components contained in products with digital elements, including by drawing up a software bill of materials in a commonly used and machine-readable format covering at the very least the top-level dependencies of the products". | IMPORTANT PRODUCTS (EUR-Lex, Annex III, Important products with digital elements): Class I item "11. Operating systems"; Class II item "1. Hypervisors and container runtime systems that support virtualised execution of operating systems and similar environments". | PENALTIES (EUR-Lex, penalties article): "Non-compliance with the essential cybersecurity requirements set out in Annex I and the obligations set out in Articles 13 and 14 shall be subject to administrative fines of up to EUR 15 000 000 or, if the offender is an undertaking, up to 2,5 % of the its total worldwide annual turnover for the preceding financial year, whichever is higher."
corrections: No correction needed; all six sub-claims match the sources. Minor wording notes (non-substantive): the SBOM text reads "at the very least" (claim paraphrases as "at least") and "commonly used and machine-readable format" (claim says "machine-readable"); both faithfully captured. On "important products": operating systems and hypervisors are BOTH listed in Annex III ("Important products with digital elements"), but in different classes — operating systems are Class I (item 11) and hypervisors/container runtime systems are Class II (item 1). The claim's "an OS plus hypervisor is a regulated 'important product'" is accurate that each is an important product, though they sit in separate classes rather than forming a single combined product category.
